perturbation.original_text: 2.4 "Current Good Manufacturing Practices" or "cGMP(s)" shall mean the standards required by the Regulatory Authority for the manufacturing, testing and quality control of pharmaceutical materials, which practices are current on the Effective Date of this Agreement and may be supplemented, amended or modified by such regulatory authority from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 "Current Good Manufacturing Practices" or "cGMP(s)" shall mean the standards CPDC deems appropriate for the manufacturing, testing and quality control of pharmaceutical materials. These practices are fixed as of the Effective Date of this Agreement and will not be updated or modified regardless of changes by any regulatory authority.
perturbation.explanation: This change freezes the definition of cGMP to the effective date, preventing updates mandated by regulatory authorities. This introduces a direct conflict between the contractual terms and actual cGMP requirements which are constantly evolving.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.100 requires that written procedures be established, followed, and updated, including any deviations. By stating that cGMPs are fixed, the contract contradicts the need for continuous improvement and adaptation mandated by federal law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text freezes the definition of cGMP, preventing updates mandated by regulatory authorities, creating a conflict. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 211.100 requires written procedures to be updated, including deviations. The modified contract directly contradicts this requirement for continuous adaptation mandated by federal law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (eCFR), indicating the existence of detailed regulations that would supersede the contract's fixed definition of cGMP. Therefore, the contract creates a legal contradiction by conflicting with established and evolving regulatory standards, undermining the adaptability required by law.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Indemnification 10.1 By CPDC. CPDC shall defend, indemnify and hold FUSION and its Affiliates and sublicensees, and each of their respective directors, officers and employees, harmless from and against any and all damages, liabilities, costs and expenses (including the reasonable costs and expenses of lawyers and other professionals) (collectively "Losses") incurred by FUSION in connection with any claim, demand, action or other proceeding (each, a "Claim") by a third party (excluding FUSION'S Affiliates and sublicensees), to the extent such Losses arise out of (a) failure of the Product delivered under this Agreement to conform to the Specifications; (b) CPDC's breach of this Agreement, including without limitation any failure of its representations and warranties set forth in Section 5.1 or 5.2 to have been accurate when made or any breach of the covenants set forth in this Agreement; or (c) the gross negligence or intentional misconduct of CPDC or any of its Affiliates, or any of their respective directors, officers, employees, provided CPDC will not have an indemnification obligation with respect to any Claim to the extent that FUSION has an indemnification obligation under Section 10.2.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Indemnification 10.1 By CPDC. CPDC shall defend, indemnify and hold FUSION and its Affiliates and sublicensees, and each of their respective directors, officers and employees, harmless from and against any and all damages, liabilities, costs and expenses (including the fixed costs and expenses of lawyers and other professionals as determined by CPDC) (collectively "Losses") incurred by FUSION in connection with any claim, demand, action or other proceeding (each, a "Claim") by a third party (excluding FUSION'S Affiliates and sublicensees), to the extent such Losses arise out of (a) failure of the Product delivered under this Agreement to conform to the Specifications; (b) CPDC's breach of this Agreement, including without limitation any failure of its representations and warranties set forth in Section 5.1 or 5.2 to have been accurate when made or any breach of the covenants set forth in this Agreement; or (c) the gross negligence or intentional misconduct of CPDC or any of its Affiliates, or any of their respective directors, officers, employees, provided CPDC will not have an indemnification obligation with respect to any Claim to the extent that FUSION has an indemnification obligation under Section 10.2.
perturbation.explanation: This change redefines 'Losses' to include 'fixed costs' instead of 'reasonable costs.' This contradicts standard indemnification principles, where costs must be reasonable and substantiated. This introduces uncertainty about which expenses are covered.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Reasonableness of Damages
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law, as reflected in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, implies a principle of reasonableness in assessing damages. By replacing reasonable costs with fixed costs determined solely by CPDC, the contract undermines the principle of reasonable and fair compensation, potentially making the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable costs' to 'fixed costs as determined by CPDC' contradicts the principle of reasonableness implied in contract law. While there is no specific law snippet provided that explicitly states 'reasonableness' in indemnification, the Law Explanation correctly asserts that general contract law implies reasonableness in assessing damages. By allowing CPDC to unilaterally determine these costs, the modified contract undermines fair compensation and makes the clause potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 13. FORCE MAJEURE. NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE FOR FAILURE TO PERFORM, OR DELAY IN THE PERFORMANCE OF, ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT (OTHER THAN PAYMENT OBLIGATIONS) WHEN SUCH FAILURE OR DELAY IS CAUSED BY AN EVENT OF FORCE MAJEURE. FOR PURPOSES OF THIS AGREEMENT, AN EVENT OF FORCE MAJEURE MEANS ANY EVENT OR CIRCUMSTANCE BEYOND THE REASONABLE CONTROL OF THE AFFECTED PARTY. INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO, WAR, INSURRECTION, RIOT, FIRE, FLOOD OR OTHER UNUSUAL WEATHER CONDITION, EXPLOSION. ACT OF GOD, PERIL OF THE SEA, STRIKE, LOCKOUT OR OTHER INDUSTRIAL DISTURBANCE, SABOTAGE, ACCIDENT, EMBARGO, BREAKAGE OF MACHINERY OR APPARATUS, INJUNCTION, ACT OF GOVERNMENTAL AUTHORITY, COMPLIANCE WITH GOVERNMENTAL ORDER ON NATIONAL DEFENSE REQUIREMENTS, OR INABILITY TO OBTAIN FUEL, POWER, RAW MATERIALS, LABOR OR TRANSPORTATION FACILITIES. IF, DUE TO ANY EVENT OF FORCE MAJEURE, EITHER PARTY SHALL BE UNABLE TO FULFILL ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT (OTHER THAN PREVIOUSLY ACCRUED PAYMENT OBLIGATIONS FROM COMPLETED WORK, THE AFFECTED PARTY SHALL IMMEDIATELY NOTIFY THE OTHER PARTY OF SUCH INABILITY AND OF THE PERIOD DURING WHICH SUCH INABILITY IS EXPECTED TO CONTINUE AND SHALL USE COMMERCIALLY REASONABLE EFFORTS TO MITIGATE THE LENGTH AND EFFECT OF SUCH FORCE MAJEURE EVENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. FORCE MAJEURE. NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE FOR FAILURE TO PERFORM, OR DELAY IN THE PERFORMANCE OF, ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT (OTHER THAN PAYMENT OBLIGATIONS) WHEN SUCH FAILURE OR DELAY IS CAUSED BY AN EVENT OF FORCE MAJEURE. FOR PURPOSES OF THIS AGREEMENT, AN EVENT OF FORCE MAJEURE MEANS ANY EVENT OR CIRCUMSTANCE BEYOND THE AFFECTED PARTY'S CONTROL. INCLUDING ONLY WAR AND ACT OF GOD. IF, DUE TO ANY EVENT OF FORCE MAJEURE, EITHER PARTY SHALL BE UNABLE TO FULFILL ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT (OTHER THAN PREVIOUSLY ACCRUED PAYMENT OBLIGATIONS FROM COMPLETED WORK, THE AFFECTED PARTY SHALL IMMEDIATELY NOTIFY THE OTHER PARTY OF SUCH INABILITY AND OF THE PERIOD DURING WHICH SUCH INABILITY IS EXPECTED TO CONTINUE AND SHALL USE COMMERCIALLY REASONABLE EFFORTS TO MITIGATE THE LENGTH AND EFFECT OF SUCH FORCE MAJEURE EVENT.
perturbation.explanation: By drastically limiting the definition of Force Majeure to include ONLY 'war and act of god,' the contract now contradicts the common understanding and legal precedent surrounding force majeure events. This makes events that would normally excuse performance (e.g., strikes, natural disasters besides 'act of God') no longer valid.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-615 - Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-615 allows for excused performance when unforeseen circumstances make performance impracticable. The modified contract's extremely narrow definition of force majeure contradicts the UCC's allowance for a broader range of unforeseen circumstances, increasing the risk of breach.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Force Majeure' to only 'war and act of God,' while the original text includes a broader range of events. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-615 allows for excused performance when unforeseen circumstances make performance impracticable. By limiting 'Force Majeure,' the contract now contradicts the UCC's allowance for a broader range of unforeseen circumstances, increasing the risk of breach as per the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also supports that the altered text contradicts the common understanding and legal precedent surrounding force majeure events, making events that would normally excuse performance no longer valid.
